source.journal_name: Romanian Journal of Information Science and Technology

paper.title: A Homomorphic Property of the Cryptosystems Based on Word Problem
paper.abstract: Discusses homomorphic property of public key cryptosystems based on word problem. Uses finitely presented partially commutative groups. Applies homomorphic encryption to electronic voting schemes. Probabilistic encryption via relator insertion.

verdict.summary: Cryptographic construction using group-theoretic word problem. All formal definitions (free partially commutative groups, Thue systems, word problem) are necessary for the cryptosystem construction and security arguments. Homomorphic property proof requires formal machinery.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Public key cryptosystem using word problem in finitely presented groups
**Level:** 4 (Cryptographic construction with algebraic proofs)
**Rationale:** Novel cryptographic construction requiring group-theoretic foundations and security properties.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 6 (alphabet, reduced word, concurrency relation, group presentation, Thue system, code)
- Theorems: 0 explicit, but construction correctness implied
- Proofs: Implicit via example verification
- Equations: Multiple (group operations, morphism properties)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~18
Expected range (Level 4): 30-80
Ratio: 0.2x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - formalism is actually conservative for cryptographic construction.

### Necessity Test
- Definition 3.1 (reduced word): NECESSARY - foundational for word problem
- Definition 3.2 (concurrency relation): NECESSARY - defines group structure
- Definition 3.3 (group presentation): NECESSARY - core of cryptosystem
- Definition 3.4 (Thue system): NECESSARY - undecidability for security
- Definition 3.5 (free partially commutative group): NECESSARY - trapdoor construction
- Definition 3.6 (code): NECESSARY - unique factorization for decryption

**All formal elements pass Necessity Test.**

### Issues
None identified. Paper acknowledges known attack [12] on the system.

---

## Citation Analysis

Total references: 23. No self-citations detected. References appropriate for cryptography/formal language theory.

---

## Structural Analysis

Clear structure: Introduction, Homomorphic Encryption background, PKC construction, E-voting application, Conclusion. Acknowledges vulnerability to chosen ciphertext attack. No artifacts provided.

---

## Conclusion

Solid cryptographic construction paper with appropriate formalism for the algebraic complexity involved. The group-theoretic definitions are necessary for the word problem-based trapdoor. Paper honestly acknowledges known attack and suggests future work.

paper.title: Multihanded and Near Perfect Self Assembling of Tile Assembling Systems
paper.abstract: Presents multiple handed diamond tile assembly models for discrete Sierpinski triangle. Generalizes two-handed assembly to h-handed assembly. Constructs 6H-DAM achieving near perfect assembly with scale factor 1 using 34 tile types. Also presents 3H-DAM with scale factor 3.

verdict.summary: DNA tile self-assembly theory paper. Formal definitions of tiles, assembly systems, stability, and near-perfect assembly are all necessary for proving the main theorems about Sierpinski triangle construction.

papers.provenance:

**Level:** 4 (Self-assembly theory with proofs)

## Conclusion

Strong theoretical contribution to tile self-assembly. Formalism is necessary and proportionate. Proves near-perfect assembly with optimal scale factor 1 for Sierpinski triangle, improving on prior work.

paper.title: Internally Expandable Pushdown Automata and Their Computational Completeness
paper.abstract: Defines internally expandable pushdown automata (IEPDA) that expand symbols deeper in pushdown rather than only at top. Proves equivalence with state grammars, therefore computational completeness. Shows IEPDAs with 4 states are computationally complete.

verdict.summary: Formal language theory paper proving computational completeness of new automaton model. All definitions, lemmas, and proofs are necessary for establishing equivalence with state grammars and Turing completeness.

papers.provenance:

paper.title: Picture Scanning Automata and Group Actions on Pictures
paper.abstract: Systematically discusses finite automata on rectangular arrays with different scanning strategies. Shows all 32 variants describe only two language classes. Uses dihedral group D4 as proof tool. Studies boustrophedon and returning finite automata.

verdict.summary: Picture language theory using group actions. The dihedral group D4 composition table and formal automaton definitions are necessary for proving that 32 scanning variants collapse to 2 language families.

papers.provenance:

---

---

## Conclusion

Elegant use of group theory to simplify picture automata classification. The D4 formalism is precisely the right tool and enables the main result that 32 variants collapse to 2 families.

paper.title: 3D DNA Origami Map Structure Simulation
paper.abstract: Presents simulation of DNA origami construction of Romanian map nanoscale structure using caDNAno and Maya. Follows Rothemund's DNA origami methodology. Proof-of-concept embedding number 100 for Romanian centenary.

verdict.summary: DNA origami proof-of-concept simulation paper. Minimal formalism - describes software workflow (caDNAno, Maya) for generating Romania map nanostructure. No theorems or proofs; appropriate for simulation/application paper.

paper.title: On a Special Variant of Rauzy Graphs
paper.abstract: Introduces half range Rauzy graphs (HRR) as variant of Rauzy graphs for word representation. Studies structural properties for infinite words of form x^omega where x is primitive. Proves every component is a cycle and gives conditions for strong connectivity.

verdict.summary: Combinatorics on words paper introducing new graph variant. All definitions (HRR graphs, primitivity) and theorems (cycle structure, strong connectivity) are necessary for establishing the graph-theoretic properties of word representations.

paper.title: Palindromic Properties of Two-Dimensional Fibonacci Words
paper.abstract: Investigates palindromic occurrences in 2D Fibonacci arrays. Extends 1D Fibonacci word palindrome study to 2D. Counts distinct palindromic subarrays (F(m) x F(n)). Studies palindromes in conjugacy class of Fibonacci arrays.

verdict.summary: Combinatorics paper on 2D Fibonacci arrays. All definitions (2D arrays, palindrome types, Fibonacci arrays) and counting theorems are necessary for establishing the palindrome enumeration results.

paper.title: On k#$-Rewriting Systems
paper.abstract: Introduces k#$-rewriting systems extending #-rewriting systems with pushdown memory. Demonstrates infinite hierarchy of language families based on rewriting position limits. Proves equivalence with k-limited state grammars.

verdict.summary: Formal language theory paper introducing new rewriting system variant. Complex definitions and proofs are necessary for establishing the infinite hierarchy characterization and equivalence with state grammars.

paper.title: The Unique Satisfiability Problem from a Membrane Computing Perspective
paper.abstract: Provides uniform polynomial time solution to UNIQUE SAT problem using polarizationless P systems with active membranes and division rules. Uses minimal cooperation and minimal production in object evolution rules. UNIQUE SAT is DP-complete.
keywords: polarizationless P systems with active membranes

verdict.summary: Membrane computing paper solving UNIQUE SAT. The P system definition and stage-by-stage analysis are necessary for proving the polynomial-time solution. Complexity class DP properly introduced.

paper.title: Accepting H-Array Splicing Systems and Their Properties
paper.abstract: Extends accepting splicing systems to H-array splicing on rectangular arrays. Compares accepting H-array splicing systems with local array systems and (Regular:Regular) array grammars. Studies uniform and non-uniform iterated splicing.

verdict.summary: 2D formal language theory extending splicing to arrays. All definitions (array splicing, domino rules, accepting systems) and comparison theorems are necessary for establishing computational power relationships.

paper.title: A Quick Survey of Tissue-Like P Systems
paper.abstract: Survey of tissue-like P systems. Presents definitions and variants including cell division, cell separation, evolutional symport/antiport. Reviews Turing universality and computational efficiency results. Discusses complexity classes and frontiers between efficiency/non-efficiency.

verdict.summary: Comprehensive survey of tissue P systems. Formal definitions and theorem statements are necessary for accurately summarizing the field's results on universality and complexity.

batch_notes: Volume 21 Issue 3 (2018) analyzed with RUIN Framework v2.0 and Necessity Test. **No formalism theater detected.** This issue focuses heavily on formal language theory, automata, and membrane computing - all Level 3-5 topics requiring substantial formalism. Papers 593-596, 598-603 are theoretical CS with appropriate formal machinery. Paper 597 (DNA origami simulation) has minimal formalism appropriate for Level 1-2 application. Final: 0 CRITICAL, 0 LIMITED, 11 ADEQUATE, 0 STRONG.
